questions.questionText: The type of epithelium lining the urinary bladder is:
questions.explanation: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) lines the urinary tract from renal calyces to the urethra. It is uniquely adapted to stretch — umbrella cells on surface have plaques of uroplakin proteins. When relaxed: 5-6 cell layers thick; when distended: 2-3 layers. Pseudostratified ciliated columnar lines the trachea. Stratified squamous lines skin, esophagus, vagina. Reference: Ross Histology — Epithelial Tissue.

questions.questionText: Goblet cells are most abundant in which part of the intestine?
questions.explanation: Goblet cells (mucin-secreting, unicellular glands) increase in number from duodenum to colon. The colon has the highest concentration as it needs more mucus for lubrication of formed feces. Conversely, Paneth cells and villi decrease distally (absent in the colon). Crypts of Lieberkühn are present throughout the small and large intestine. Reference: Ross Histology — GI Tract.

questions.questionText: Hassall's corpuscles are characteristic of which organ?
questions.explanation: Hassall's corpuscles (thymic corpuscles) are concentric whorls of epithelial reticular cells found exclusively in the thymic medulla. They are involved in regulatory T-cell (Treg) development. The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ (T-cell maturation). It is largest relative to body size at birth, largest absolute size at puberty, then involutes (replaced by fat). Reference: Ross Histology — Lymphoid Tissue.

questions.questionText: The cell that produces collagen in connective tissue is the:
questions.explanation: Fibroblasts are the principal cells of connective tissue, responsible for synthesizing collagen (type I is most common), elastic fibers, and ground substance. Mast cells release histamine and heparin. Macrophages are phagocytic (derived from monocytes). Plasma cells produce antibodies (derived from B-lymphocytes). Fibroblasts are most active during wound healing. Reference: Ross Histology — Connective Tissue.

questions.questionText: Which type of collagen is found in basement membranes?
questions.explanation: Type IV collagen forms the network structure of basement membranes. It does NOT form fibers (non-fibrillar). Type I: most common (bone, skin, tendon, fascia). Type II: cartilage, vitreous humor. Type III: reticular fibers (liver, spleen, lymph nodes; also in early wound healing). Goodpasture's syndrome involves antibodies against type IV collagen (glomerular and alveolar basement membranes). Reference: Ross Histology — Collagen Types.

questions.questionText: Nissl bodies (rough endoplasmic reticulum) are found in neurons in the:
questions.explanation: Nissl bodies (basophilic aggregates of rough ER) are present in the neuronal cell body (soma/perikaryon) and dendrites, but ABSENT from the axon and axon hillock. They are responsible for protein synthesis. Chromatolysis (dissolution of Nissl bodies) occurs after axonal injury — cell body swells, nucleus moves peripherally. This is the hallmark of a neuronal reaction to injury. Reference: Ross Histology — Nervous Tissue.

questions.questionText: The glomerular filtration barrier consists of all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Glomerular filtration barrier has 3 layers: (1) Fenestrated endothelium (blocks cells), (2) Glomerular basement membrane/GBM (blocks large proteins), (3) Podocyte foot processes with slit diaphragms (final barrier). Mesangial cells are supportive/contractile cells between capillary loops — they regulate filtration rate and phagocytose immune complexes but are NOT part of the filtration barrier. Reference: Ross Histology — Kidney.

questions.questionText: The cells in the islets of Langerhans that produce insulin are:
questions.explanation: Islets of Langerhans: Beta cells (60-70%) → insulin; Alpha cells (20-25%) → glucagon; Delta cells (5-10%) → somatostatin; PP cells (F cells) → pancreatic polypeptide. Beta cells are the most numerous and are centrally located. Type 1 DM = autoimmune destruction of beta cells. Type 2 DM = insulin resistance + relative beta cell failure. Reference: Ross Histology — Pancreas.

questions.questionText: The type of cartilage found in the epiglottis and pinna of the ear is:
questions.explanation: Elastic cartilage: epiglottis, pinna (auricle), external acoustic meatus, corniculate and cuneiform cartilages. It contains a dense network of elastic fibers (seen with Verhoffs stain) along with type II collagen. Hyaline: tracheal rings, nasal septum, ribs, articular surfaces. Fibrocartilage: intervertebral disc, pubic symphysis, menisci, TMJ disc. Reference: Ross Histology — Cartilage.

questions.questionText: Intercalated discs are characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Intercalated discs are specialized junctional complexes unique to cardiac muscle, connecting adjacent cardiomyocytes. They contain: (1) Fascia adherens (like zonula adherens — anchors actin), (2) Desmosomes (resist mechanical stress), (3) Gap junctions (electrical coupling — allow action potential propagation). Gap junctions make cardiac muscle a functional syncytium. Reference: Ross Histology — Muscle Tissue.

questions.questionText: Kupffer cells are specialized macrophages found in the:
questions.explanation: Kupffer cells are resident macrophages (part of the mononuclear phagocyte system) found in the lumen of hepatic sinusoids. They phagocytose bacteria, debris, and old RBCs from portal blood. Other specialized macrophages: alveolar macrophages (lungs), microglia (brain), osteoclasts (bone), Langerhans cells (skin), mesangial cells (kidney). Reference: Ross Histology — Liver.

questions.questionText: The zona glomerulosa of the adrenal cortex produces:
questions.explanation: Adrenal cortex layers (from outside in): Zona glomerulosa → mineralocorticoids (aldosterone). Zona fasciculata → glucocorticoids (cortisol). Zona reticularis → androgens (DHEA). Memory aid: 'GFR — Salt, Sugar, Sex' or 'Go Find Rex — Makes Good Sex.' Adrenal medulla (chromaffin cells) → catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine). Reference: Ross Histology — Adrenal Gland.

questions.questionText: The stain used to demonstrate reticulin fibers (type III collagen) is:
questions.explanation: Reticulin fibers (type III collagen) are demonstrated by silver impregnation stains (Gordon-Sweet, Gomori). They appear black. PAS stain demonstrates glycogen, basement membranes, and mucus (magenta color). Masson's trichrome stains collagen blue/green and muscle red. Congo red stains amyloid (apple-green birefringence under polarized light). Reference: Ross Histology — Special Stains.

questions.questionText: The cells that line the sinusoids of the spleen and are responsible for filtering blood are:
questions.explanation: The splenic sinusoids are lined by specialized endothelial cells called littoral cells (sinus-lining cells). They have intercellular slits that allow flexible, normal RBCs to pass through but trap old, rigid, or abnormal RBCs (culling function). The spleen filters blood (unlike lymph nodes which filter lymph). White pulp = lymphoid tissue around central arterioles. Red pulp = sinusoids + splenic cords. Reference: Ross Histology — Spleen.

questions.questionText: Peyer's patches are aggregated lymphoid follicles found in the:
questions.explanation: Peyer's patches are aggregated lymphoid follicles found in the submucosa (NOT mucosa) of the ileum, on the anti-mesenteric border. They are part of GALT (gut-associated lymphoid tissue). M cells (microfold cells) overlie them, sampling antigens from the intestinal lumen. Peyer's patches are the site of primary pathology in typhoid fever. They are also involved in intussusception (as lead points in older children). Reference: Ross Histology — Ileum.

questions.questionText: The type of epithelium found in the cornea is:
questions.explanation: The corneal epithelium is stratified squamous non-keratinized (5-7 cell layers). It is one of the body's most rapidly regenerating tissues. Other non-keratinized stratified squamous locations: esophagus, vagina, oral cavity, oropharynx, anal canal (below pectinate line). Keratinized stratified squamous: skin (epidermis). Reference: Ross Histology — Eye.

questions.questionText: Osteoclasts are derived from:
questions.explanation: Osteoclasts are multinucleated giant cells derived from the monocyte-macrophage lineage (hematopoietic stem cells). They resorb bone using acid (H+ via carbonic anhydrase) and enzymes (TRAP, cathepsin K) in Howship's lacunae. Osteoblasts (from mesenchymal stem cells) form bone. Bisphosphonates and denosumab (anti-RANKL) inhibit osteoclasts. Paget's disease has giant osteoclasts (>10 nuclei). Reference: Ross Histology — Bone.

questions.questionText: The type of capillary found in the liver sinusoids and bone marrow is:
questions.explanation: Sinusoidal capillaries (discontinuous) have large gaps between endothelial cells AND discontinuous basement membrane — allowing passage of large molecules and blood cells. Found in: liver, spleen, bone marrow. Fenestrated capillaries have pores with intact BM (kidney glomeruli, endocrine glands, intestinal villi). Continuous capillaries have no fenestrae (muscle, CNS — BBB, lungs). Reference: Ross Histology — Blood Vessels.

questions.questionText: The brush border (microvilli) of the proximal convoluted tubule is best demonstrated by which stain?
questions.explanation: PAS (Periodic Acid-Schiff) stain demonstrates glycoproteins and is excellent for showing: brush border of PCT, basement membranes, glycogen (in liver/muscle), mucin (goblet cells), fungal cell walls. PAS with diastase digests glycogen (so remaining PAS-positive material is glycoprotein, not glycogen). The brush border increases absorptive surface area 20-fold in the PCT. Reference: Ross Histology — Kidney Staining.

questions.questionText: Club-shaped (Clara) cells that secrete surfactant-like material are found in the:
questions.explanation: Clara cells (now called club cells) are non-ciliated secretory cells found predominantly in terminal and respiratory bronchioles. They secrete a surfactant-like material (CC16/uteroglobin) and also metabolize toxins via cytochrome P450. Alveolar type II pneumocytes produce true surfactant (dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine). Clara cells are the progenitor cells of the bronchiolar epithelium. Reference: Ross Histology — Respiratory System.

questions.questionText: The Haversian system (osteon) is the structural unit of:
questions.explanation: The osteon (Haversian system) is the basic structural unit of compact (cortical) bone. It consists of concentric lamellae surrounding a central Haversian canal (containing blood vessels and nerves). Adjacent osteons communicate via Volkmann's canals (perpendicular). Osteocytes lie in lacunae and communicate via canaliculi. Spongy bone has trabeculae without Haversian systems. Reference: Ross Histology — Bone Structure.

questions.questionText: Melanocytes are derived from:
questions.explanation: Melanocytes are derived from neural crest cells (neuroectoderm). They reside in the basal layer (stratum basale) of the epidermis and produce melanin (in melanosomes) which is transferred to surrounding keratinocytes. Melanin absorbs UV radiation, protecting DNA. Melanoma arises from malignant transformation of melanocytes. Vitiligo = autoimmune destruction of melanocytes. Reference: Ross Histology — Skin.

questions.questionText: The cells responsible for bone formation are:
questions.explanation: Osteoblasts (from mesenchymal stem cells) are the bone-forming cells. They secrete osteoid (type I collagen and ground substance), which then mineralizes. When trapped in their secreted matrix, they become osteocytes. Osteoblasts express RANKL (stimulating osteoclasts) and are targets of PTH signaling. Alkaline phosphatase is a marker of osteoblastic activity. Reference: Ross Histology — Bone Cells.

questions.questionText: Sebaceous glands are associated with which type of secretion?
questions.explanation: Sebaceous glands use holocrine secretion — the entire cell disintegrates to release its product (sebum). Eccrine/merocrine: sweat glands, pancreas, salivary glands (secretory vesicles fuse with membrane). Apocrine: mammary glands (debated), specialized sweat glands in axilla/groin (the apical portion pinches off). Sebaceous glands are found everywhere except palms and soles. Reference: Ross Histology — Skin Appendages.

questions.questionText: Space of Disse in the liver is located between:
questions.explanation: The space of Disse (perisinusoidal space) lies between the sinusoidal endothelium and hepatocytes. It contains hepatic stellate (Ito) cells that store vitamin A. In liver fibrosis/cirrhosis, Ito cells transform into myofibroblasts and deposit collagen in the space of Disse (capillarization), impeding nutrient exchange. Bile canaliculi are between adjacent hepatocytes (separate compartment). Reference: Ross Histology — Liver Microanatomy.

questions.questionText: The layer of the epidermis where keratinocytes undergo apoptosis and lose their nuclei is the:
questions.explanation: In the stratum granulosum, keratohyalin granules and lamellar bodies are prominent. Keratinocytes undergo apoptosis here, losing their nuclei and organelles, becoming the dead cornified cells of the stratum corneum. Lamellar bodies release lipids that form the waterproof barrier. Stratum lucidum (only in thick skin of palms/soles) is between granulosum and corneum. Reference: Ross Histology — Epidermis.

questions.questionText: Type II pneumocytes in the alveoli are responsible for:
questions.explanation: Type II pneumocytes (granular pneumocytes) produce pulmonary surfactant (mainly DPPC — dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine). Surfactant reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse. They also serve as progenitor cells for Type I pneumocytes. Type I pneumocytes (95% of alveolar surface) perform gas exchange. Type II are cuboidal, fewer in number, but more numerous as cells. NRDS in premature infants = surfactant deficiency. Reference: Ross Histology — Alveoli.

questions.questionText: Brunner's glands, which secrete alkaline mucus to neutralize gastric acid, are found in the:
questions.explanation: Brunner's glands are unique to the duodenum and are located in the submucosa (NOT mucosa — this is a key distinguishing feature). They secrete alkaline mucus (rich in bicarbonate) to neutralize gastric acid entering the duodenum. Brunner's gland hyperplasia/adenoma can occur. Esophageal glands are also submucosal. Paneth cells (in crypts) secrete defensins/lysozyme. Reference: Ross Histology — Duodenum.
